- Mark the point loads to scale on the chart where you think the worst case is likely to be.
- Read off the co-efficient on the chart. Multiply each load by the corresponding co-efficient.
- Divide the whole lot by (8.pi) as it says on top of the chart.
- The result is value in kNm/m.

- http://www.aucklanddesignmanual.co.nz/regulations/codes-of-practice/stormwatercodeofpractice/guidance/design/designcriteria
- Mladen
- Mark Groves
- Some else asked this the other day. Generally there is NZTA Specification P46 to cover SW design for new NZTA roads. However, bridge deck drainage also has its own nuances which isn't specifically covered in P46.
- Requirements about keeping flow out of the live lanes stands on a bridge same as anywhere else.
- We now generally try and treat the run-off from the bridge as well, rather than discharging over the side direct to the river. The latter is harder to consent and generally not something mana whenua are keen on either.
- Examples like the Taramakau or Ahaura Bridge, the AC is used to form a gutter between the AC pavement and the barrier. This is sized to convey a WQ flow (typically designed to convey 80% of the annual run-off volume) back to and beyond the abutment/s for treatment. Any excess flow is then spilt through the barrier via letter box openings which keeps down the scale of the gutter needed. The movement joint at the abutment th

- You also need to decide if you are looking at a “building” or not. – covered by Sections 8 and 9 of the Building Act.
- Note Section 17: All building work must comply with the building code, whether or not a building consent is required in respect of that building work.
- In terms of consent requirements this is covered in; Schedule 1 Building work for which building consent not required: Part 1 Exempted building work. Specifically the clauses around network utility operators, unoccupied buildings and TA.
- Finally if on reading the Building Code Clauses you have listed you need further guidance; you might want to check out a couple of DOC (Department of Conservation) documents:
